specification.topic-statement: The completion layer renders tab-completion candidates as a list of terminal rows, highlighting the selected entry with reverse-video styling and padding each row to the full terminal width.

scope.in-scope: Visibility determination from an external completion context pointer, height computation from the candidate count, per-candidate row rendering with description lookup, selection highlighting, width padding, and line termination.
scope.boundary: The candidate list, candidate count, and current selection index are managed by the tab completion subsystem. This topic receives them via a live pointer and renders the result.
Command descriptions are retrieved from an external registry at render time. This topic uses the description string that is returned; it does not manage the registry.

How the layer's visibility, height, and render functions are called by the rendering pipeline is covered by the Layer Rendering specification. This topic covers only the implementations of those three functions for the completion layer.
The rendering pipeline calls the visibility predicate once per frame. If it returns false, height and render are not called. If true, height is called to measure the layer, and render is called with a terminal width, a zero-indexed start row within the layer, and a row count to render.

field.name: current
field.note: zero-indexed position of the selected candidate

field.name: description
field.note: human-readable description shown next to the candidate

behavior.visibility: The layer is visible when the completion context pointer dereferences to a non-null value. When the pointer dereferences to null, the layer is invisible and produces no rows.
behavior.height-calculation:
When invisible, height is zero. When visible, height equals the candidate count from the completion context.
The terminal width parameter is accepted by the height function but is not used in any calculation. Candidate count alone determines height regardless of terminal width.
behavior.rendering:
The number of candidates rendered is the lesser of the supplied row count and the total candidate count. Candidates are rendered starting from index zero regardless of the start_row parameter.
The start_row parameter, which would normally indicate an offset into the layer's row space for viewport clipping, is accepted but never used. Rendering always begins from candidate index zero.
Before rendering any row, the full command registry is queried to obtain all command records (name and description pairs).
For each candidate at index i (from 0 to candidates_to_render minus 1):
1. If i equals the current selection index, the ANSI sequence for reverse video combined with bold (ESC[7;1m) is appended to the output.
2. Two spaces are appended.
3. The candidate name string (all bytes) is appended.
4. Three spaces are appended.
5. The description is resolved: the command registry records are scanned linearly. The first record whose name matches the candidate name exactly provides the description. If no record matches, an empty string is used as the description.
6. The description string (all bytes) is appended.
7. If i equals the current selection index, the ANSI reset sequence (ESC[0m) is appended.
8. Padding: a visible length is computed as 2 plus the byte length of the candidate name plus 3 plus the byte length of the description. While this visible length is strictly less than the terminal width, one space is appended and the visible length is incremented.
9. The ANSI erase-to-end-of-line sequence (ESC[K) followed by carriage-return and line-feed (\r\n) is appended.
ANSI escape sequences within a candidate name or description would be counted as bytes in the visible length, causing the padding to undershoot. Candidate names and descriptions sourced from the command registry are plain text, so this does not affect normal operation.
When the combined byte length of the candidate row equals or exceeds the terminal width, the padding loop does not execute and the content is not truncated. The line will extend beyond the terminal width.
